title: Chumor 2.0: Towards Benchmarking Chinese Humor Understanding
research_questions: How can a Chinese humor explanation benchmark be constructed from culturally specific Ruo Zhi Ba jokes?
How well do current Chinese-capable LLMs judge whether an explanation fully explains a Chinese joke?
Have LLMs achieved human-level understanding of Chinese humor?
Does chain-of-thought prompting improve LLM performance on Chinese humor explanation classification?
Can GPT-4o and ERNIE4-turbo explain jokes as well as humans?

datasets_used.name: Ruo Zhi Ba (RZB)
datasets_used.size: Best Annual Threads between 2018 and 2021; all threads in the Moderator’s Recommendation section

datasets_used.name: Human explanations for Chumor case study
datasets_used.size: 1,951 jokes; mean of 78 Chinese characters of explanation per joke; total length 151,730 Chinese characters

methods_proposed.name: Chumor 2.0
methods_proposed.summary: A Chinese humor explanation dataset sourced from Ruo Zhi Ba jokes. GPT-4o and ERNIE4-turbo generated explanations, and five native Chinese-speaking authors annotated whether each explanation fully explained the joke.

methods_proposed.name: Humor explanation classification task
methods_proposed.summary: Given a joke and a proposed explanation, a model must choose whether the explanation is “fully explain” or “partially/does not explain.” The paper evaluates models with direct prompting and chain-of-thought prompting.

methods_proposed.name: Human-vs-LLM explanation preference study
methods_proposed.summary: For 1,951 jokes, one author wrote human explanations and GPT-4o and ERNIE4-turbo generated two-sentence explanations. Six native Chinese-speaking college students performed A/B preference annotation comparing human and LLM explanations.

humor_theories.detail: The paper categorizes jokes into cultural, situational, pun-based, homophonic, glyph-based, and cross-lingual types, but does not cite or operationalize a named humor theory.

evaluation_methods.detail: Five native Chinese-speaking authors manually annotated GPT-4o and ERNIE4-turbo explanations as either “fully explain the joke” or “partially explain or not explain the joke,” using majority vote.
LLM predictions were compared with Chumor labels using accuracy, false positive rate, false negative rate, and Matthews Correlation Coefficient; no response was marked incorrect.
Three Chinese native speakers unfamiliar with the work annotated a randomly chosen subset of 200 examples to estimate human performance.

Six native Chinese-speaking college students conducted A/B preference annotation between human and LLM joke explanations; each joke received three preference annotations, majority vote determined winning rate, and all-disagree cases were labeled “Undecided”.

headline_results.finding: Chumor contains 3,339 humor explanation classification instances, with the paper stating 1,454 good and 1,887 bad explanations.
The best LLM accuracy was 60.3%, achieved by ERNIE4-turbo and Gemini1.5-pro, far below human performance of 78.3%.
The best MCC score achieved by LLMs was 0.29, compared with a human average of 0.60.
Chain-of-thought prompting often hurt performance; ERNIE4-turbo accuracy decreased from 60.3% to 45.2%, and eight of the ten LLMs’ MCC scores decreased under CoT prompting.
headline_results.value: 60.3% to 45.2%; eight of the ten LLMs

headline_results.finding: In A/B preference evaluation, Figure 4 reports GPT-4o explanations at 59.9% Human wins, 31.8% Tie, 2.2% LLM wins, and 6.2% Undecided; ERNIE4-turbo at 53.7% Human wins, 38% Tie, 2.9% LLM wins, and 5.3% Undecided.
headline_results.dataset: Human explanations for Chumor case study

headline_results.finding: In sampled explanation error analysis, GPT-4o had more cultural-unawareness errors than ERNIE4-turbo, while ERNIE4-turbo had more contextual and pun-based errors.
headline_results.value: Cultural: 29.5% vs 10.5%; Contextual: 22.0% vs 26.0%; Pun-based: 15.5% vs 19.5%

key_findings: Chumor is presented as the first Chinese humor explanation dataset and is sourced from Ruo Zhi Ba, a Chinese Reddit-like platform with intellectually challenging and culturally specific jokes.
All ten evaluated LLMs performed poorly on the explanation classification task, with accuracy ranging between 44.6% and 60.3%.
Human annotators substantially outperformed LLMs, achieving 78.3% average accuracy and 0.60 MCC on a 200-example subset versus the best LLM results of 60.3% accuracy and 0.29 MCC.
Chain-of-thought prompting did not reliably help Chinese humor understanding and often increased false positives or false negatives.
Model performance varied by joke type, with situational jokes easier than some cultural, homophonic, pun-based, glyph-based, and cross-lingual cases.
Human-written joke explanations were strongly preferred over GPT-4o and ERNIE4-turbo explanations in the A/B study.
limitations: Due to limited budget and API access, the paper could not evaluate all possible LLMs.
The research focuses primarily on demonstrating that humor understanding remains challenging, rather than solving the problem.
Although the authors filtered out excessively offensive RZB content, some jokes may still be perceived as offensive because humor is subjective.
Preference annotation involved subjective humor interpretation, with a combined average agreement percentage of 61.4%.
A model response with no generated answer due to safety protocols or filtering sensitive terms was marked incorrect; this occurred 21 times and exclusively with GLM-4plus.
safety_ethics_notes: The authors state that they filtered out excessively offensive content, but some jokes may still offend individuals with different cultural or personal standards. They recommend using Chumor with cultural sensitivity and caution, especially in applications involving diverse audiences or sensitive topics.

human_samples.population: Ruo Zhi Ba (RZB) forum users/posters whose public web content supplied the jokes
human_samples.recruitment: Existing RZB content: previously crawled “Best Annual Threads” from 2018–2021 and all threads in the “Moderator’s Recommendation” section
human_samples.observations: Public source threads/posts; after cleaning, the paper reports 3,339 joke-explanation instances in Chumor and a case-study corpus covering 1,951 jokes.
human_samples.detail: The paper does not report the number or demographics of original RZB posters.

human_samples.observations: Manual labels for the generated explanation instances in Chumor, using majority vote; reported labels are 1,454 good and 1,887 bad explanations for 3,339 instances.
human_samples.detail: They annotated whether each GPT-4o or ERNIE4-turbo explanation “fully explain[s] the joke” or “partially explain[s] or not explain[s] the joke.” The reported good/bad counts do not sum to the reported total.

human_samples.population: Chinese native speakers unfamiliar with the work
human_samples.observations: Annotated a randomly chosen subset of 200 examples for the human-performance comparison; paper reports average accuracy 78.3% and MCC 0.60.
human_samples.detail: No further demographics, compensation, individual scores, or agreement statistics are reported.

human_samples.observations: Wrote/annotated human explanations for 1,951 jokes; mean length 78 Chinese characters per joke; total length 151,730 Chinese characters.
human_samples.detail: These human explanations are used in the A/B case study comparing human-written explanations against GPT-4o and ERNIE4-turbo explanations.

human_samples.population: Native Chinese-speaking college students who grew up in China
human_samples.observations: A/B preference annotations comparing human explanations with one LLM explanation for each joke; each joke received three preference annotations; combined average agreement 61.4%.
human_samples.detail: The paper says each annotator read around 300k Chinese characters. Appendix A conflicts with the main text by listing named authors as preference annotators.

human_samples.population: An individual not involved in writing the original explanations
human_samples.observations: Error-type analysis on a sample of 200 examples for GPT-4o and ERNIE4-turbo explanations; examples could have multiple error types.
human_samples.detail: The paper does not report this annotator’s credentials, blinding, or reliability checks.

judges.detail: Five native Chinese-speaking authors provided the reference good/bad labels for Chumor explanation instances by majority vote.

judges.name: Accuracy, false positive rate, false negative rate, Matthews Correlation Coefficient
judges.detail: Model predictions were compared against Chumor reference labels; no-response or incorrect responses were marked incorrect.
Three Chinese native speakers unfamiliar with the work annotated 200 randomly selected examples to estimate human performance on the same explanation-classification task.

Six native Chinese-speaking college students judged A/B preferences between human and LLM explanations. Appendix C says the human and LLM explanations were randomly assigned as Explanation 1 and Explanation 2.
An individual not involved in writing the original explanations conducted the error-type analysis for GPT-4o and ERNIE4-turbo explanations on 200 sampled examples.

Authors manually cleaned the RZB data, removed non-joke/offensive/incomplete/philosophical threads, removed duplicates, and categorized jokes into six types; detailed protocols and reliability are not reported.

generation_selection.scope: Chumor dataset explanation generation for benchmark instances
generation_selection.n: Not explicitly reported; the paper describes generated explanations from GPT-4o and ERNIE4-turbo and no best-of-N selection.
generation_selection.detail: Generated explanations were not selected by an automatic or human selector before labeling; instead, the generated explanation instances were manually labeled good/bad.

generation_selection.scope: LLM classification responses under direct prompting and chain-of-thought prompting
generation_selection.n: One response per model per item per prompting setting is implied; repeated sampling is not reported.
generation_selection.detail: The paper says each prompt was input to the model and responses were collected; majority voting or best-of-N over multiple model outputs was not used.

generation_selection.scope: Case-study joke explanation generation for A/B preference comparison
generation_selection.n: One two-sentence explanation per LLM per joke is described for GPT-4o and ERNIE4-turbo.
generation_selection.detail: The LLMs were prompted: “Please explain the joke in two sentences: [Joke].” No multiple candidates or selection procedure is reported.

model_provenance.name: Yi_34B
model_provenance.detail: Open-source model family from 01.AI; paper says the instruction-tuned version was used, but exact checkpoint/revision, deployment method, and inference date are not reported. Reference model card accessed 2024-12-10.

model_provenance.name: Nemotron_70B
model_provenance.detail: Open-source NVIDIA model; exact Hugging Face revision/snapshot, deployment method, and inference date are not reported. Reference model card accessed 2024-12-10.

model_provenance.name: Athene_70B
model_provenance.detail: Open-source Nexusflow model; exact checkpoint/revision, deployment method, and inference date are not reported. Reference model card accessed 2024-12-10.

model_provenance.name: Qwen2.5_72B
model_provenance.detail: Open-source Alibaba/Qwen model; exact checkpoint revision, deployment method, and inference date are not reported. Reference model card accessed 2024-12-10.

model_provenance.name: Mistral_123B
model_provenance.detail: Paper labels the evaluated model as Mistral_123B and cites the Mistral-Large-Instruct-2407 model card. Exact revision/snapshot, deployment method, and inference date are not reported; model card accessed 2024-12-10.

model_provenance.name: Gemini1.5-pro
model_provenance.detail: Closed-source Google model; API/service details, exact snapshot, and inference date are not reported. Documentation accessed 2024-12-10.

model_provenance.name: GLM-4_plus
model_provenance.detail: Closed-source BigModel/Tsinghua model; exact snapshot, access route, and inference date are not reported. Documentation accessed 2024-12-10.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4_turbo
model_provenance.detail: Closed-source OpenAI model; exact API snapshot/version and inference date are not reported. OpenAI Researcher Access credit supported GPT experiments.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o
model_provenance.detail: Used both to generate explanations and as an evaluated classifier/explanation generator. Exact API snapshot/version and inference date are not reported. OpenAI Researcher Access credit supported GPT experiments.

model_provenance.name: ERNIE_4-turbo
model_provenance.detail: Used both to generate explanations and as an evaluated classifier/explanation generator. Exact service snapshot and inference date are not reported; documentation accessed 2024-12-10.

inference_budgets.scope: Chumor classification evaluation across ten LLMs under DP and CoT
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: One call per model per prompt setting per instance is implied; repeated calls are not reported.
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 1 response per call; no best-of-N or majority vote over repeated model outputs reported.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: Limited budget and API access are mentioned; GPT experiments were supported by OpenAI Researcher Access credit, but no cost, hardware, or compute totals are reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Evaluation used 3,339 Chumor instances. A response was correct if it matched the reference label; incorrect or missing responses were marked incorrect. Missing/no-response cases occurred 21 times, exclusively with GLM-4plus.

inference_budgets.scope: Generation of Chumor candidate explanations by GPT-4o and ERNIE4-turbo
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: Not reported; the paper does not report multiple candidates or selection.
inference_budgets.detail: The prompt, sampling parameters, token limits, number of failed generations, and exact call accounting for the benchmark-explanation generation step are not reported.

inference_budgets.scope: Case-study generation of two-sentence explanations by GPT-4o and ERNIE4-turbo
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: One prompt per LLM per joke is implied, but exact call accounting is not reported.
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 1 explanation per LLM per joke is described.
inference_budgets.detail: Prompt is reported in Chinese and English: “Please explain the joke in two sentences: [Joke].” The case study covers 1,951 jokes.

human_baselines.description: Three Chinese native speakers unfamiliar with the work labeled a random 200-example subset; paper reports human average accuracy 78.3% and MCC 0.60 versus best LLM accuracy 60.3% and MCC 0.29.
human_baselines.detail: Humans and LLMs performed the same label-judgment task against the same reference-label scheme, but the human baseline was only on a 200-example subset while headline LLM scores are reported for the full 3,339-instance benchmark; same-subset LLM scores are not reported.

human_baselines.description: One author wrote human explanations for 1,951 jokes; six native Chinese-speaking college students compared those explanations against GPT-4o and ERNIE4-turbo explanations in randomized A/B preference judgments.
human_baselines.detail: Human and LLM explanations were evaluated for the same jokes by the same preference procedure. LLMs were prompted to produce two-sentence explanations consistent with the human-explanation format, though the human authoring process is less fully specified.

mitigations: The labeled Chumor benchmark was newly constructed for the paper rather than reused as an existing public benchmark.
Reference labels were manually assigned by native Chinese-speaking authors.
The authors manually cleaned the public RZB source data, including removing duplicates and non-joke/offensive/incomplete/philosophical threads.
contamination_risk.basis: The released Chumor benchmark and labels appear newly created, but the underlying RZB jokes come from public web/forum content, including 2018–2021 Best Annual Threads and Moderator’s Recommendation threads, which likely predate several evaluated models and may have been present in web-scale pretraining data. The paper does not report any decontamination test, training-data search, or model-specific contamination control. Additional uncertainty comes from evaluating GPT-4o and ERNIE4-turbo on explanation instances generated by those same models.

llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Primary benchmark labels were assigned by five native Chinese-speaking authors; a separate human baseline used three Chinese native speakers; A/B explanation-quality claims used six native Chinese-speaking college-student preference annotators.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: LLMs generated candidate explanations and were evaluated systems, but no LLM was used as the evaluator, metric, or judge for the paper’s headline conclusions.

reporting_gaps: Exact API snapshots, model revisions, inference dates, and access/deployment routes are not reported for the evaluated models.
Sampling parameters such as temperature, top-p, max tokens, stop conditions, seeds, and retry policies are not reported for classification or explanation generation.
The prompt used to generate the original Chumor benchmark explanations from GPT-4o and ERNIE4-turbo is not reported; only the case-study two-sentence explanation prompt is shown.
The paper reports 3,339 instances but 1,454 good plus 1,887 bad labels, which sum to 3,341.
The paper does not fully explain how 1,951 jokes produced 3,339 explanation instances, or whether missing/filtered generations account for the difference from two explanations per joke.
Inter-annotator agreement, vote distributions, adjudication details, and source blinding are not reported for the five-author Chumor good/bad labeling.
Recruitment, compensation, demographics beyond native-speaker status, individual scores, and agreement are not reported for the three-person human baseline.

Preference-study reporting is inconsistent: the main text says six native Chinese-speaking college students performed A/B annotation, while Appendix A lists seven named authors as conducting the preference annotations.
For the preference study, recruitment and compensation are not reported; source blinding is only inferable from the Explanation 1/Explanation 2 randomization instructions.
The joke-type categorization process and reliability are not reported.
The 200-example error-analysis sampling method, annotator credentials, blinding, and reliability checks are not reported.
No contamination or decontamination analysis is reported despite public RZB source material predating the evaluated models.
The introduction text states LLM winning rates of 6.2% for GPT-4o and 5.3% for ERNIE4-turbo, while Figure 4 labels those values as Undecided and shows LLM wins of 2.2% and 2.9%.
